summary: Soaring Dark Star, tight Jack Straw open a magical Boston farewell
blurb: The band launches the night with a crisp Jack Straw that sets a high‑energy tone. A luminous Dark Star sequence dominates the second set, flowing into Saint Of Circumstance and Eyes Of The World. Overall the performance is fluid, with Hornsby’s keyboards adding extra sparkle.
review:
On September 26, 1991 the Grateful Dead closed their Boston Garden run with a show that still feels fresh after three decades. The first set kicks off strong – Jerry’s soaring guitar on Jack Straw rides a gorgeous Bruce Hornsby‑Vince Welnick piano/accordion blend, and the band rolls through Cold Rain And Snow, Candyman and Mexicali Blues without missing a beat. The real fireworks erupt in the second set: Dark Star erupts into Saint Of Circumstance and then blossoms into an expansive Eyes Of The World, all underpinned by Phil’s melodic bass and a tight rhythm wall from Mickey and Billy. The Other One returns for another Dark Star trek before the band lifts off with Attics Of My Life and a rousing Good Lovin'. Even the encore, though marred by an over‑bearing accordion on Brokedown Palace, ends the night on a heartfelt “And We Bid You Good Night.” Across dozens of recordings the performance is consistently praised for its clarity, tight transitions and emotive solos – making this Boston farewell one of the late‑’90s highlights.

For listeners seeking the clearest window into that night, the Miller‑remastered soundboard (gd1991-09-26.sbd.miller.76050.sbeok.flac16) delivers crystal‑clear detail and captures every nuance of Hornsby’s keyboards, Jerry’s tone and the band’s dynamic flow.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Across fifteen detailed analyses, eleven are positive and four mixed; no outright negative sentiment appears. Reviewers repeatedly call the show "stellar," "magical" and "tight," especially praising Dark Star, Jack Straw and Hornsby’s keyboards. The only consistent criticism is the aggressive accordion on Brokedown Palace, which does not outweigh the abundant praise.

best_recording.reason: Miller‑remastered soundboard with crystal‑clear mix; captures the full energy and nuance of the performance without dropouts.

key_highlights: Jack Straw opener shines with Hornsby’s expansive keyboards.
Dark Star > Saint Of Circumstance > Eyes Of The World sequence is luminous and tightly woven.
Second‑set transitions (The Other One into Dark Star, Attics Of My Life) showcase the band’s improvisational fire.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers soaring, emotive solos throughout – from the crisp lead on Jack Straw to the gorgeous tone that carries Dark Star and Eyes Of The World.
band_performance.Phil: Provides melodic, supportive bass lines; his interaction lifts the band especially during The Other One and the second‑set jams.
band_performance.Bob: Vocals are passionate and guitar work adds depth; noted for adding a “Tibetan‑inspired” flavor on certain passages.
band_performance.Brent: Bruce Hornsby’s piano/keyboard work is a highlight, supplying lush chords and dynamic runs that propel Jack Straw, Candyman and the Dark Star suite. Vince Welnick’s contributions blend in, keeping the overall texture balanced despite occasional accordion over‑reach on Brokedown Palace.
band_performance.Mickey: Drumming is cerebral and builds intensity, underpinning the extended improvisations with subtle accents.
band_performance.Billy: Provides a solid, driving backbone; his interplay with Mickey keeps the rhythmic flow tight during high‑energy moments.
